coredata.dc:title: Charge transfer mediated photoluminescence enhancement in carbon dots embedded in TiO2 nanotube matrix
coredata.dc:creator: Babusenan, Anu
Pandey, B.
Roy, Somnath C.
Bhattacharyya, Jayeeta
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have drawn a lot of attention in recent years for imaging applications due to their luminescence properties in the visible range. There has been a strong thrust in achieving CQDs with enhanced luminescence efficiency. We report nearly three times increase in the total photoluminescence emission of CQD films by depositing them on TiO2 nanotube (NT) substrates, instead of glass. The net increase in PL arose from higher CQD core emission and the appearance of a strong surface state emission. High-resolution X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to analyze the composition of the CQD surfaces, which confirmed more carboxyl groups on the surface of CQD in the CQD:NT composite. The band alignment of the composite, determined from XPS measurements, favoured carrier transfer from TiO2 NT to CQD. The TiO2 NT, having better surface coverage and wide absorption spectrum, had high absorption cross-section. The efficient transfer of the carriers from NT to CQD is found to be the dominant phenomenon leading to a significant increase in the PL emission intensity.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) A schematic diagram of the steps involved in the synthesis of CQD on TiO2 NT substrate. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

TEM images of carbon dots in 100 nm scale (a) and 10 nm scale (b). Inset shows lattice fringes of the dots from HRTEM and (c) and (d) are the size distributions of the particles with Gaussian fit. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) SEM image of the top surface of TiO2 nanotube and the inset showing the diameter of a typical tube. (b) Absorption spectra of TiO2 NT and CQD (c) The PL spectra obtained from CQD and TiO2 NT for 350 nm excitation. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
2D plots of PL emission intensity for different excitation and emission wavelengths for (a) CQDs on glass and (b) CQD on TiO2 NT. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

PL spectra of (a) CQD on glass and (b) CQD on TiO2 NT for excitation at 350 nm. The measured data (circles) were fitted by sum (grey line) of two and three Gaussians, respectively, indicated by dashed (peak A), dotted (peak B) and solid (peak SS) lines. The corresponding variations in PL peak position are plotted for (c) CQD on glass and (d) CQD:NT composite. Peaks A, B and SS are denoted by squares, circles and stars, respectively. Excitation and emission wavelength are denoted by λex and λem. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

The variation of the PL peak intensities, for the three fitted Gaussians, with excitation wavelength for (a) peak A, (b) peak B and (c) peak SS, for CQD on glass (triangles) and CQD:TiO2 NT composite (circles).

High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s in (a) CQD on glass and (b) CQD:NT composite. The shaded regions are the deconvoluted contributions from CC/C–C, C–N/C–O, CO and COOH bonds (from lower to higher binding energy). The survey scan of XPS in CQD on glass(c) and the composite (d).

A schematic of the band alignment in CQD:TiO2 NT composite. Cases for PL excitation at (a) 325 nm (3.8 eV) and (b) 400 nm (3.1 eV) marked as dashed vertical arrows are shown. Solid vertical arrows are the transitions giving rise to the PL peaks. The solid curved arrows indicate carrier transfers. All energies shown are in eV. The typical emission spectra corresponding to excitation wavelengths of (c) 325 nm and (d) 400 nm are plotted. The solid and dashed lines correspond to CQD on glass and CQD:TiO2 NT composite, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Charge transfer mediated photoluminescence enhancement in carbon dots embedded in TiO2 nanotube matrix

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Indian Institute of Technology Madras, Chennai, 600036, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Indian Institute of Technology Madras, Chennai, 600036, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have drawn a lot of attention in recent years for imaging applications due to their luminescence properties in the visible range. There has been a strong thrust in achieving CQDs with enhanced luminescence efficiency. We report nearly three times increase in the total photoluminescence emission of CQD films by depositing them on TiO2 nanotube (NT) substrates, instead of glass. The net increase in PL arose from higher CQD core emission and the appearance of a strong surface state emission. High-resolution X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to analyze the composition of the CQD surfaces, which confirmed more carboxyl groups on the surface of CQD in the CQD:NT composite. The band alignment of the composite, determined from XPS measurements, favoured carrier transfer from TiO2 NT to CQD. The TiO2 NT, having better surface coverage and wide absorption spectrum, had high absorption cross-section. The efficient transfer of the carriers from NT to CQD is found to be the dominant phenomenon leading to a significant increase in the PL emission intensity.

ce:section.ce:para: High luminescence efficiencies and properties like biocompatibility, low toxicity and strong chemical inertness have made carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a viable candidate for the applications in biomedical imaging, biomolecule sensing, printing inks, sensors and light emitters [1–4]. Such applications demand highly luminescent CQDs. CQDs can be synthesized economically using techniques such as the hydrothermal reaction of organic sources, oxidation of carbon-based materials and microwave-assisted synthesis [5,6]. The CQD emissions are stable, and covers a wide spectral range, spanning the whole visible spectrum, extending from near UV to NIR [7–9]. Extensive research is going on to increase the efficiency and tune the frequency of emission from CQD ensembles, which is desirable for a wide range of optoelectronic and biomedical applications. Novel synthesis methods, surface passivation, surface plasmon resonance and doping or co-doping with different elements are some of the approaches employed to improve the luminescent properties of CQDs [10–13]. Surface functionalization can also tune the surface states, which can contribute to the control and increase of the emission from CQDs [13–16].

Most of these methods aim to enhance radiative recombination rates. However, one of the bottlenecks is the low overall absorption cross-section of the CQD thin films due to their small surface coverage. The optimized luminescence is limited by the initial carrier generation. In this work, we demonstrate the enhancement of photoluminescence (PL) from CQDs by coupling them on a suitable nanostructured oxide matrix.

We investigated PL emission efficiencies of CQDs deposited on TiO2 nanotubes (NTs). TiO2 has found applications for its photocatalytic activities. Composites of TiO2 with different materials have been investigated to improve its photocatalytic performances [17–20]. The CQD:TiO2 nanostructure composites are well-studied systems, where the CQDs were shown to play the dual role of donating electrons to TiO2, as well as acting as electron acceptors in certain cases [21–26]. This lead to the enhancement in photocatalytic activities and photocurrent in TiO2 NTs. Earlier works reported efficient electron transfers from CQDs to TiO2 nanorods, sensitizing the photocatalytic process in the visible wavelength range [24,25]. However, depending on the energy level alignment, electron transfer from TiO2 to CQDs was also observed [26]. The photo-generated electrons from the TiO2 NTs were transferred to the CQDs, resulting in a reduction of the recombination of electron-hole pairs in TiO2, thereby increasing their photocatalytic efficiencies. Exploiting the possibility of charge transfer between TiO2 NT and CQD, we explored the other side of the story, where the carrier transfer was used to facilitate increased light emission from the CQD ensemble. The novelty of this work lies in the fact that we considered CQD as the primary material in the composite, where the TiO2 NT was used to improve the emissive properties of the dots significantly.

We used vertically aligned separated NT structures where the intertube separation not only gave better surface to volume ratio, but also allowed an easy access to outer surfaces of the nanotubes. The NTs are shown to absorb light over a wide wavelength range [27]. In the subsequent sections, we present our experimental results where a significant increase in the PL from the CQDs have been observed. Moreover, the surface state emission, which was otherwise dormant in the CQD thin films on glass, were activated. The detailed analysis of the band alignment of this composite was found to support the carrier transfer processes, which we propose to be the primary mechanism responsible for the PL enhancement.

Carbon dots were synthesized using hydrothermal method. First, 2.0 g of citric acid was dissolved in a mixture of ethanol (25 mL) and formamide (25 mL) by ultrasonication for 5 min at room temperature. The solution was transferred to a 100 ml, Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 120 °C for 5 hours. During this process, the transparent solution gradually changed the color to magenta. The solution was then cooled down to room temperature and was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min. The resulting supernatant contained nitrogen-doped carbon nanoparticles having a diameter ranging from 2 to 20 nm [28]. We fabricated vertically aligned TiO2 nanotubes by electrochemical anodization of titanium foils [27]. The anodization was done at a constant voltage of 60 V for 2 hours with Ti and Pt foils as anode and cathode, respectively. The electrolyte consisted of a mixture of 96 ml di-ethylene glycol (DEG), 0.6 wt% ammonium bi-fluoride salt (NH4F·HF) and 4 ml de-ionized water. The as-prepared amorphous TiO2 NT layer grown on Ti foil was annealed in air, at 450 °C for 3 hours [27]. The annealed NTs were used as templates for the deposition of CQDs by drop-casting technique. The solutions were filtered with a 0.22 μm filter attached to a syringe before drop-casting. Equal volume of CQDs (5 μL) was also deposited on a cleaned glass substrate, used as reference. Samples were kept at 80°C for 1 hour to obtain a completely dried film. A detailed schematic diagram of the sample preparation is given in Fig. 1 .

The morphology of the CQDs was studied using transmission electron microscopy (Model: JEOL 3010,300 kV). We used a reduced voltage of 200 kV to avoid sample damage. Fig. 2 a and b show a broad size distribution of the as-grown CQDs. The lattice fringes in the inset of Fig. 2b show the lattice spacing of about 0.34 nm corresponding to the (002) plane, evident from the XRD plot (Fig. S1). Fig. 2(c) and (d) show a histogram of the carbon dot diameters obtained from the analysis of TEM images of CQDs situated at different positions on the grid. The diameter of the dots ranged from 2 nm to 20 nm, with two subsets of dots. The bimodal distribution was fitted with two Gaussian functions having mean diameters around 3.7 nm and 11 nm. The TiO2 nanotube samples were imaged (Fig. 3 a) using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, FEI Quanta 400). The tubes were well separated with an average diameter of about 300 nm (Fig. 3a, inset) and length ∼2 μm (cross-sectional SEM image in Fig. S2). Raman spectra of NT confirmed the anatase phase of TiO2 (Fig. S3). The elemental analysis using energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) measurements of the composite showed signatures corresponding to Ti, O, N and C, which confirmed the presence of CQD and TiO2 NT on the composite sample (Fig. S4). Element mapping on the CQD:NT film revealed uniform distribution of carbon on the substrate (Fig. S5).

Absorption spectra were measured with Xenon lamp as the broadband source in a Bentham IPCE PVE 300 system using the DTR6 integrating sphere. For the PL measurement, the sample was excited using a Xenon lamp, and the typical excitation intensity used was ∼2.8 mW/cm2. The emitted photons were detected using a spectrometer coupled to a CCD. The elemental analysis was performed by an XPS, with PHIBOS100 energy analyzer, using Aluminium (Kα: 1486.61 eV) X-ray source. The optical properties and the analysis are presented in details in the following sections.

The absorption spectra of the TiO2 nanotube arrays and the CQDs are shown in Fig. 3b. Absorption of NT in the visible range was significantly higher compared to the CQD. The absorption around 400 nm corresponded to the bandgap transition in TiO2 [27,29]. Earlier reports demonstrated a similar enhancement in light absorption, in the visible range, for nanostructured TiO2 [30]. In the absorption spectrum of CQDs, the peak located around 350 nm (Fig. 3b), could be attributed to the core level σ → π* transition [31]. Absorption of the composite was higher than bare CQD and was dominated by the absorption spectrum of NTs (Fig. S6). PL emission from TiO2 NT was very low, with a peak around 505 nm originating from defects like oxygen vacancies (Fig. 3c) [32]. For longer wavelength excitations, no significant emission from the NT was observed. Emission intensity from CQD under identical excitation conditions was orders of magnitude higher and ranged from 400 nm to 550 nm, corresponding to the HOMO-LUMO transitions of the dot ensemble [31,32].

A comparison of the PL emission from CQDs deposited on glass and on TiO2 NT substrates is shown in Fig. 4 , where 2D plots of PL emission intensities for different emission and excitation wavelengths are presented. For CQD on glass, a distinct emission (marked as “bulk”), which underwent redshift with increasing excitation wavelength, was observed. Such dependence has been reported earlier for emissions from the carbon core of the CQDs [33,34]. When deposited on NT substrates, the PL from CQDs showed a substantial increase in intensity, along with the appearance of an additional feature around 575 nm (marked as “SS”). The peak position of this feature was independent of the excitation wavelength and was attributed to surface state emission from the CQDs [13,35]. Similar surface state emission was observed when the CQDs were dispersed in ethanol, but it disappeared for CQD films on glass. A detailed analysis of the PL spectrum of CQD on glass and TiO2 NT substrates [Fig. 5 (a) and (b)] revealed two peaks convolved in the PL emission from the core, both of which showed excitation energy-dependent shift. The shift in the PL peaks could arise when different subsets of dots were excited by different excitation frequencies. The two peaks in the PL spectrum (both originating from core of the CQDs) indicated a bimodal distribution of the quantum dot ensemble [36]. The PL spectrum was fitted with two Gaussians (A and B), as shown in Fig. 5(a). The full width at half maximum values were ∼100 nm, indicating broad size distribution of the dots. Both the PL peaks showed continuous redshift with increasing excitation wavelength [Fig. 5c]. For longer excitation wavelengths (>400 nm), the smaller CQDs were not excited and did not contribute to the PL emission. The PL spectrum was then dominated by peak B, which showed a slight increase in intensity when peak A disappeared. For PL from CQD: NT composite, three Gaussians were fitted, the third peak (SS) accounting for the surface state emission. The peaks A and B exhibited red-shift with increase in the excitation wavelength while the surface state emission (peak SS) was excitation independent, as shown in Fig. 5(d). The SS feature became more intense as the excitation wavelength was increased, and the core emission intensity decreased [Fig. 4b]. No such emission from the surface states was observed for the CQD on glass substrate.

Fig. 6 shows the comparison of the measured PL emission intensities from peaks A, B and SS, for CQDs on TiO2 NT and glass, for different excitation wavelengths. For excitation at 325 nm, there was a decrease in PL for CQD:NT composite compared to CQD on the glass. However, for longer excitation wavelengths, the intensities of all the peaks increased with the emergence of the SS emission, for the composite.

To understand the appearance of SS emission, we studied the nature of the surface states using XPS. The high-resolution XPS spectra for carbon in CQDs on glass and NTs are shown separately in Fig. 7 (a) and (b), where the Ti 2p, N1s and O1s peaks are shown in Figs. S7 and S8. The survey scan of XPS is given in Fig. 7 (c) and (d). In reference to earlier reports [37,38], the C1s band can be deconvoluted into four peaks, confirming the presence of sp2 carbons (CC, 284.5eV), sp3 carbons (C–O/C–N, 286.1eV), carbonyl groups (CO, 287.8 eV), and carboxyl groups (COOH, 289.0 eV). The spectra were calibrated using a gold (Au 4f) sample. The fitted peak at 289 eV was stronger for the CQD:NT composite compared to that on glass, indicating an increase in the amount of carboxyl groups on the surface of these CQDs. The presence of amino groups and carboxyl groups on the surface of CQDs were reported to increase surface emission from the dots [31,38,39]. Thus, one of the reasons for the appearance of SS emission from our CQDs in the CQD:TiO2 NT composite could be the increase in carboxyl groups on the surface.

Besides the increase in the carboxyl group affecting the SS emission, we propose that the primary cause for the increase of the overall PL from CQDs in the composite is carrier transfer from TiO2 NT to CQDs. To explore the possibility of such transfer processes, one needs to understand the band alignment of the composite. We adopted the method developed by Kraut et al. [40,41] to calculate the relative positions of valence band maxima of the composite using XPS. First, we performed XPS measurements of CQD on glass and bare TiO2 NT on Ti and all the core level peaks obtained from high-resolution XPS spectra were fitted using Voigt function. Then, the valence band edge (VBE) scan was done for both the samples separately, and we linearly fitted the leading edge of the VB and the flat energy distribution (Fig. S9). The point of intersection of these two lines defined the VBE energy. The differences between the core levels (peak of the Voigt function) and the VBE of C in CQD (
Δ
E
C
) and Ti in TiO2 NT (
Δ
E
T i
) were found to be 282.96 eV and 456.17 eV, respectively (Fig. S10). When measured for CQD:NT composite, the separation between the core levels of C and Ti (
Δ
E
C , T i
) was 173.68 eV. Thus the VBE of CQD w.r.t that of NT in the CQD:NT composite is given by the equation,
Δ
E
V
=
Δ
E
C , T i
+ Δ
E
C
− Δ
E
T i

The position of the VBE of CQD was calculated to be 0.47 ± 0.05 eV above that of TiO2 NT, in the composite. The conduction band edge (CBE) with respect to the VBE was obtained from absorption measurements. Tauc plots were used for average bandgap estimation [42], which gave 3.24 eV for NT and 3.48 eV for CQD, which were in good agreement with previous reports [27,43,44] [Fig. S11].

A summarized band alignment for CQD:TiO2 NT, as obtained from our measurements, is schematically shown in Fig. 8 (a and b). The three sets of transitions observed in PL correspond to the core level transitions of bimodal CQD distribution and surface state emission marked by solid lines A, B and SS. Each of the transitions is broadened according to the emission linewidth observed in PL. The two figures correspond to two typical excitation wavelengths used. Both NTs and CQDs absorb the incident light. For excitation at 325 nm (3.8 eV) the whole ensemble of CQDs absorbs, including the smaller dots. The excited levels in the dots lie above the CBE (broadened due to nano-structuring) of TiO2 [Fig. 8(a)]. Thus, there is a considerable probability of transfer of carriers from these CQDs to the NT, leading to quenching of PL of peak A corresponding to smaller CQDs, as was observed in Fig. 8(c). Peak SS, however, showed an increase even for 325 nm excitation since surface states were further below in energy and gained carriers from TiO2 NT. The intensity of peak B remained nearly unchanged [Fig. 8c]. For the longer excitation wavelengths, the transfer of carriers from NT to CQDs dominated since then the lower energy dots prevailed, most of which were below the TiO2 NT CBE. This resulted in an overall increase in PL for all the peaks A, B and SS as observed in Figs. 4 and 8(d). NTs, being an un-interrupted matrix, had high absorption, and supplied a large number of electrons and holes to the dots. We observed about three-fold increase in the total PL intensity for CQD:TiO2 NT composite compared to CQD on the glass To further confirm the charge transfer, we treated the samples with a hole scavenger (Isopropyl alcohol) and measured the PL [Fig. S12]. The emission from the CQD on the composite was reduced much more, compared to that from the CQD on glass in the presence of Isopropyl alcohol. The presence of the hole scavenger removed holes from the material and decreased radiative recombination. The reduction of PL to a higher extent for the CQD:NT composite, clearly indicated the role of the charge transfer from TiO2 NT to CQD, in the enhancement of emission from CQD.

A simple post-growth technique of depositing the CQDs on TiO2 NT matrix was employed to increase PL emission from the CQDs. TiO2 NT substrates, having high absorption over a broad spectral range, generated a large number of carriers on photoexcitation. These carriers were transferred to the CQDs due to the favorable band alignment of the composite resulting in an increase in PL emission efficiency from the core and the surface of the CQDs. Increase in the carboxyl groups on the surface of CQDs was also observed which added to the enhancement of the surface state emission of CQDs on NTs. The activation of surface state emission not only added to the increase in total emission intensity but broadened the emission spectrum. The efficient carrier transfer in the CQD:TiO2 NT composite, which influenced the PL emission, can be exploited to have brighter CQD based emitters. This work opens up new scopes to investigate substrate compatibility related to the band alignment, which can be a route to improve luminescence of CQDs.

Anu Babusenan had grown the CQDs and fabricated the composites. The characterization of the samples and optical measurements were done by AB under the guidance of Dr.Jayeeta Bhattacharyya. The analysis of the data and manuscript preparation was also done by AB and JB.
B. Pandey and Dr. Somnath C. Roy grew the TiO2 nanotubes and contributed to the discussions.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the Department of Science and Technology, India for funding the spectroscopic measurement setup under Project No. ECR/2015/000147 and the XPS at the Nano Functional Materials Technology Center (Grant No. SRNM/NAT/02–2005).
B. Pandey would like to acknowledge the financial grant (Sanction No.: SR/WOS-A/PM-97/2016) by Department of Science and Technology, Government of India under the DST Women Scientist (WOS-A) scheme.
